discovered.fit_reason: Official VictoriaMetrics MCP exposing MetricsQL queries and cluster admin. Many large-K8s shops swap Prometheus for VM; a parallel opsbench recipe widens addressable customer base without reinventing the read-path.
discovered.notes: Vendor-maintained, currently active. Treat write tools (vmalert mgmt, retention ops) as Cedar-gated.

discovered.name: grafana/loki-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Dedicated Loki MCP from Grafana for LogQL queries, label/value discovery, series enumeration. Direct path for opsbench forensic evidence sealing of log windows around an incident without going through the bigger grafana MCP surface.
discovered.notes: License field missing on the repo — needs verification before vendoring. Read-only operations; minimal allowlist surface.

discovered.name: ntk148v/alertmanager-mcp-server
discovered.fit_reason: Exposes Prometheus Alertmanager (silences, alert groups, status) over MCP. Closes a gap in opsbench's incident workflow: the agent can correlate active alerts with the kubectl/forensic evidence it already collects and propose silence windows for blast-radius control.
discovered.notes: Smaller project but the only purpose-built Alertmanager MCP. Silence create/delete tools must be Cedar-gated; read tools are safe to allowlist broadly.

discovered.name: open-telemetry/opentelemetry-collector-contrib
discovered.fit_reason: Authoritative receiver/processor/exporter catalog. opsbench can generate Collector config snippets (kubeletstatsreceiver, k8sclusterreceiver, filelogreceiver, k8sattributesprocessor) as part of a team-platform-engineering bootstrap skill so target clusters emit the signals the incident agent later queries.
discovered.notes: Huge surface; opsbench only needs a curated subset of components. No MCP — wrap as a skill that emits Collector YAML.

discovered.name: open-telemetry/opentelemetry-operator
discovered.fit_reason: Kubernetes operator for managing OTel Collector and auto-instrumentation CRDs. Future team-platform-engineering skills can shell out to kubectl apply on OpenTelemetryCollector/Instrumentation CRDs to enable evidence capture on demand during incidents.
discovered.notes: Pairs with opsbench's existing kubectl/Cedar gating. No MCP server upstream — call via kubectl from a skill.

discovered.name: vectordotdev/vector
discovered.fit_reason: High-performance log/metric pipeline. opsbench can wrap `vector` config generation/validation (vector validate, vector vrl) in a skill so the platform-engineering agent ships tuned log shippers as part of a remediation PR after an incident.
discovered.notes: Now Datadog-maintained; MPL-2.0 is permissive enough for vendoring config templates. No native MCP.

discovered.name: k8sgpt-ai/k8sgpt
discovered.fit_reason: Battle-tested CNCF CLI that analyzes K8s objects for failures (pod, deploy, ingress, hpa, etc.) and returns structured findings. opsbench's team-incident-response can shell out to `k8sgpt analyze --explain --output json` as a first-pass evidence collector before deeper kubectl forensics.
discovered.notes: Has an operator (k8sgpt-ai/k8sgpt-operator) for cluster-resident scanning. JSON output is well-defined — easy to seal into SHA-256 evidence bundles.

discovered.name: opendatahub-io/rhoai-observability-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Reference implementation that fuses Prometheus + Alertmanager + Loki + Grafana + K8s queries behind one MCP tailored to a specific workload (vLLM on OpenShift). Excellent blueprint for opsbench's own consolidated observability MCP recipe and how to scope per-workload toolsets.
discovered.notes: Low-star but a Red Hat-supported pattern; the value is in the architecture (one MCP, multiple read-only backends) not adoption.

discovered.name: ThoTischner/observability-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Unified observability gateway MCP across Prometheus, Loki and arbitrary backends with built-in cross-signal anomaly detection. Useful as either a drop-in upstream or a blueprint for the consolidated MCP opsbench may build to avoid N×M tool wiring.
discovered.notes: Very new (2026-06 activity). Worth tracking; not yet production-grade — vet anomaly-detection claims before shipping.

discovered.name: grafana/pyroscope
discovered.fit_reason: Continuous-profiling backend with HTTP API for flamegraph/profile queries. A platform-engineering skill can pull profile windows around an incident to attach CPU/heap evidence to the SHA-256-sealed bundle, complementing metrics and traces.
discovered.notes: AGPL on the server — keep opsbench integration as API client only (no vendored server code). No first-party MCP yet; wrap the query API.

trends: MCP has become the de-facto agent interface for observability in 2026 — every major vendor (Grafana, SigNoz, VictoriaMetrics, Dynatrace, Zabbix, Loki, Alertmanager) now ships an official or community MCP server, and Azure SRE Agent / AWS DevOps Agent both treat MCP as their primary investigation surface. The hot pattern is agentic SRE loops (HolmesGPT, Metoro, Komodor Klaudia, k8sgpt) that iteratively pull from Prometheus + Loki + Tempo + K8s to converge on root cause, often with eBPF as the new signal layer. Convergence is also visible: multi-backend "unified observability MCPs" (traceloop, ThoTischner, rhoai) are emerging to dodge the N×M problem of one-MCP-per-store.
gaps: No mainstream MCP today bundles evidence sealing (hash-chained metrics+logs+traces windows around an incident) — opsbench's SHA-256 evidence bundle pattern is genuinely novel and could be packaged as an "observability forensic capture" recipe other agent stacks would consume. There's also no Cedar/OPA-policy-gated MCP layer for write operations like alert rule edits or silences; every MCP today either exposes them raw or omits them, leaving a real opportunity for opsbench to publish a policy-gated proxy MCP. Finally, OTel Collector / Vector config-as-code is still hand-authored — a team-platform-engineering skill that generates and validates Collector + Vector configs from a target workload spec would close a clear ecosystem gap.

trends: In 2026 the security-scanner ecosystem is rapidly shipping first-party MCP servers (Aqua's trivy-mcp, Kubescape 4.0's mcpserver, CrowdStrike's falcon-mcp) instead of waiting for community shims, and the KAgent/in-cluster agent pattern is becoming the canonical deployment model for AI security tooling. The other dominant trend is the emergence of agent-runtime guardrails: Falco's Prempti and projects like FalcoClaw treat the AI agent itself as a workload to be policed at the syscall/tool-call boundary, with allow/deny/ask verdicts shipped as Claude Code skills. Aggregator MCPs like CloudshipAI's Ship that bundle 50+ scanners behind a single Dagger-containerized surface are also rising, betting that agents prefer a uniform toolbelt over per-tool installs.
gaps: There is no incident-response-grade scanner orchestrator that combines a Trivy/Kubescape/Grype scan, a Tetragon/Falco runtime timeline, and SHA-256 evidence sealing into a single Cedar-gated workflow - everyone ships individual MCPs but no one ties findings to chain-of-custody artifacts the way opsbench does. Cross-scanner finding normalization (one schema for Trivy + Grype + Kubescape + Falco) is still an unsolved problem with only early-alpha attempts. Cedar-policy-as-code gating on top of these MCPs is entirely absent: existing MCPs trust the agent caller, which leaves opsbench a clear opening to publish a reference secure security-MCP gateway pattern combining Cedar, per-agent allowlist, and sealed evidence that wraps any of the upstream scanners above.

discovered.name: aquasecurity/trivy-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Vendor-maintained MCP server from Aqua exposing Trivy filesystem, image, and remote-repo vulnerability scans to Claude Code natively. opsbench team-incident-response and a future team-platform-engineering can call it to triage CVEs in suspected-compromised images during a forensic incident, gated by Cedar policy and recording SHA-256 of scan output as sealed evidence.
discovered.notes: Official Aqua plugin. Stable, supports stdio/HTTP/SSE transports. Tools include scan_filesystem, scan_image, scan_repo. Plays well with the per-agent tool allowlist model already in opsbench.

discovered.name: kubescape/kubescape
discovered.fit_reason: Kubescape 4.0 ships a built-in MCP server (mcpserver subcommand) plus a KAgent-native plugin that lets agents call list_vulnerability_manifests, list_configuration_security_scan_manifests, and ApplicationProfile/NetworkNeighborhood queries against a live cluster. Exactly the surface a team-incident-response forensic agent needs to enumerate posture findings during AKS incidents.
discovered.notes: CNCF incubating, v4.0.9. The mcpserver targets in-cluster operator deployment; opsbench would call it through a kubeconfig context, evidence-seal scan JSON, and Cedar-gate which controls/clusters are visible to which agent.

discovered.name: falcosecurity/prempti
discovered.fit_reason: Falco-powered policy boundary that intercepts every Claude Code tool call (shell, file, web, MCP) with allow/deny/ask verdicts. Directly complements opsbench's Cedar-policy gating layer: Cedar authorizes the intent, Prempti enforces runtime syscall-level rules. Bundled ruleset already covers credential exfil, sandbox-disable, and MCP/skill poisoning - all opsbench threat models.
discovered.notes: Experimental preview (v0.2.1). Ships a Claude Code skill for authoring Falco rules. Rust binary; runs Linux/macOS/Windows. Strong dual-layer story when paired with opsbench's existing per-agent allowlists.

discovered.name: CrowdStrike/falcon-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Vendor MCP exposing 20+ Falcon modules including Kubernetes container analysis, vulnerability scans, CSPM asset inventory, RTR (real-time response), and quarantine. For opsbench customers running Falcon, the team-incident-response agent can pivot from a Kubescape posture finding to Falcon detections, host quarantine, and IOC enrichment with one MCP.
discovered.notes: Public preview, v0.11.0. Container-ready (quay.io image), supports streamable-http. Needs Falcon API credentials; opsbench should sealed-secret them and Cedar-gate destructive actions (quarantine, contain) behind explicit human approval.

discovered.name: cloudshipai/ship
discovered.fit_reason: Single MCP framework that bundles Trivy, Grype, Syft, Dockle, Cosign, Hadolint, Kubescape, Kube-bench, Falco, Kyverno, Trufflehog, Gitleaks, Prowler, Checkov, Terrascan, Semgrep, Nuclei, etc. - all run in isolated Dagger containers. Lets opsbench expose a one-stop security toolbelt to team-incident-response without standing up each scanner individually.
discovered.notes: Apache-2.0, Go, v0.9.1. Containerized execution avoids polluting the host - aligns with opsbench's evidence-sealing model since each scan is reproducible. Slightly lower stars but actively maintained; a candidate to vendor as the team-platform-engineering scanner blueprint.

discovered.name: thnkbig/FalcoClaw
discovered.fit_reason: Open-source runtime response engine extending Falco syscall detection with automated response actions tailored for AI agents and Linux. Useful blueprint for the next opsbench feature: tying a Falco detection to a Cedar-gated remediation action (kill process, isolate pod) with evidence sealing of the full event chain.
discovered.notes: Very new project. Worth tracking for response-action patterns rather than direct dependency. Verify license header before vendoring.

discovered.name: cilium/tetragon
discovered.fit_reason: Production-ready eBPF runtime security with in-kernel filtering, process/network/file tracing, and policy enforcement - CNCF, v1.4 February 2026. No native MCP yet, but a team-incident-response agent can shell out to tetra (the CLI) or read the JSON event stream via a thin opsbench wrapper for forensic timeline reconstruction.
discovered.notes: No MCP upstream - wrap with HKUDS/CLI-Anything. Stars approximate. Heavy infra (kernel eBPF), so the opsbench skill should assume Tetragon is pre-deployed and only expose query/policy surface, not install.

discovered.name: aquasecurity/kube-bench
discovered.fit_reason: CIS Kubernetes Benchmark scanner. No native MCP, but opsbench can wrap kube-bench run --json as a skill: team-incident-response uses it to capture a CIS posture snapshot at incident time and seal the SHA-256 as evidence. Trivy already integrates the same checks, so kube-bench is the lighter standalone option.
discovered.notes: Mature, stable, and offline-capable. Use sparingly - Trivy now covers most kube-bench checks, so this is the fallback when only the standalone tool is acceptable in the customer env.

discovered.name: anchore/grype
discovered.fit_reason: Vulnerability scanner pairing with Syft for SBOM. No first-party MCP, but Grype is the de-facto alternative when customers won't run Trivy. opsbench can wrap it as a skill: take an image ref or SBOM, return CVEs+CVSS, seal the JSON. Useful diversity to avoid single-scanner trust.
discovered.notes: Pair with anchore/syft for SBOM generation step. Both Apache-2.0. No MCP upstream - CLI-anything wrap opportunity.

discovered.name: hackersatyamrastogi/pentesting-cyber-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Catalog of 50+ MCP security servers (Nmap, Burp, Nuclei, Shodan, BloodHound, Semgrep, Trivy). Useful as a discovery reference for which scanners already have MCP shims, but the toolkit itself leans pentest/offensive, not the defensive incident-response posture opsbench targets.
discovered.notes: Use as a survey doc, not a dependency. Many components are early-stage shims by community authors; vet individually before adoption.

discovered.name: Horizon-Digital-Engineering/security-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Unified MCP wrapping Snyk, SonarCloud, Semgrep, Trivy, gosec, npm audit with a single normalized schema and a whats_wrong_with_my_repo aggregator. The normalization pattern is the interesting bit for opsbench: one consistent finding shape across heterogeneous scanners, which simplifies Cedar policy authoring.
discovered.notes: Early alpha, most adapters are stubs. Worth copying the unified-finding schema concept rather than depending on the code.

discovered.name: kagent-dev/kagent
discovered.fit_reason: CNCF Sandbox project for running AI agents natively in Kubernetes with MCP tool servers for Kubernetes, Istio, Helm, Argo, Prometheus, Grafana, Cilium. Kubescape 4.0 ships a KAgent plugin, so adopting KAgent CRD patterns lets opsbench team-incident-response agents live inside the cluster they investigate.
discovered.notes: Active (v0.9.5 June 2026). Strong reference architecture for in-cluster agent deployment with Tool Server CRDs - opsbench could expose a KAgent-compatible Tool Server wrapping its evidence-sealing pipeline.

discovered.name: nirmata/kyverno-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Vendor-published MCP server that exposes Kyverno policy management (apply, validate, list policies, get violations) directly to Claude Code/MCP-aware agents. Lets team-incident-response and team-platform-engineering query and remediate AKS/K8s admission violations during forensic loops without shelling out.
discovered.notes: AGPL-3.0 is copyleft — fine to call as a separate MCP server, but do not vendor source. Maintained by Nirmata (Kyverno commercial steward). Pairs naturally with the existing AKS forensic recipe.

discovered.name: cedar-policy/cedar-for-agents
discovered.fit_reason: Official AWS/CNCF project. Ships cedar-analysis-mcp-server, mcp-tools-sdk (Rust), and cedar-policy-mcp-schema-generator that auto-generates Cedar schemas from MCP tool descriptions. Directly extends opsbench's existing Cedar-policy gating: schema-gen lets us auto-derive per-agent allowlists from MCP tool surfaces.
discovered.notes: Apache-2.0, multi-crate. The schema generator is the highest-value piece for opsbench's per-agent tool allowlist enforcement.

discovered.name: open-policy-agent/gatekeeper
discovered.fit_reason: The canonical OPA-based K8s admission controller. opsbench incident-response agents should be able to read ConstraintTemplates, list violations, and propose new Constraint YAML during AKS forensic loops; a thin CLI wrapper around kubectl + gator gives that surface today.
discovered.notes: Includes the gator CLI for offline constraint testing, perfect for sealed-evidence policy replay. Has an AGENTS.md file in repo suggesting upstream agent-readiness.

discovered.name: open-policy-agent/conftest
discovered.fit_reason: Battle-tested CLI for running Rego policies against K8s manifests, Terraform, Dockerfiles, Tekton, SBOMs. team-platform-engineering can wrap `conftest test` as a deterministic gate; output is JSON and SHA-256-sealable as opsbench evidence.
discovered.notes: No MCP wrapper yet — opportunity to ship a thin one via HKUDS/CLI-Anything. Stable, widely deployed.

discovered.name: vectimus/vectimus
discovered.fit_reason: Cedar-based deterministic policy engine that intercepts agent tool calls across Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, Gemini, LangGraph, ADK. Directly mirrors opsbench's Cedar-policy gating model and ships 78 Cedar policies / 369 rules mapped to CVEs and SOC2/NIST/ISO/EU AI Act — high-quality template-blueprint plus possible upstream policy import.
discovered.notes: Sister repo vectimus/policies (Apache-2.0) is a directly importable Cedar policy catalog. Very young (pre-1.0) but actively maintained.

discovered.name: sondera-ai/sondera-coding-agent-hooks
discovered.fit_reason: Reference implementation of per-agent Cedar-policy hook adapters for Claude/Cursor/Gemini/Copilot pre-tool-use lifecycle. Exactly the architecture opsbench needs to unify Cedar gating across multiple agent hosts — normalized event types (ShellCommand, FileRead, FileWrite), Cedar evaluation, Allow/Deny/Escalate verdicts.
discovered.notes: Strong star momentum from HN. Tarpc RPC over Unix sockets — heavier than opsbench currently needs but the hook-normalization layer is portable.

discovered.name: stacklok/toolhive
discovered.fit_reason: Enterprise MCP gateway with per-tool policy enforcement, OIDC auth, K8s-native CRDs. Could host opsbench's MCP recipes in a hardened gateway for team-incident-response, providing audit + RBAC layer above Cedar gating.
discovered.notes: Very active. Use as the runtime substrate when opsbench deployments need centralized MCP governance.

discovered.name: styrainc/regal
discovered.fit_reason: Linter and LSP for Rego. team-platform-engineering can shell `regal lint` over generated Rego (e.g. policies an LLM drafts during incident response) to catch bugs before deploy. Useful CI gate too.
discovered.notes: Maintained by Styra (OPA creators). Fast, stable, easy to wrap.

discovered.name: IBM/mcp-context-forge
discovered.fit_reason: Open-source MCP/A2A/REST federation registry with RBAC, OAuth, OTel observability. Useful as a reference for opsbench's per-agent tool-allowlist plumbing and federation across multiple MCP recipes; the gateway pattern is reusable even without built-in OPA/Cedar.
discovered.notes: v1.0 GA. Pairs Cedar/OPA externally rather than embedding. Reasonable baseline architecture.

discovered.name: tomjwxf/scopeblind-gateway
discovered.fit_reason: Stdio MCP proxy that evaluates Cedar policies on tool calls and Ed25519-signs decisions as offline-verifiable receipts. This is a near-exact analog of opsbench's Cedar-gating + SHA-256 evidence-sealing pipeline — worth studying for the signed-decision pattern.
discovered.notes: Small but architecturally aligned. Author claims IETF Internet-Draft. Cross-check before deeper dependence.

discovered.name: permitio/permit-mcp-gateway
discovered.fit_reason: Commercial MCP authorization proxy using OPA + Zanzibar ReBAC. Reference for how external PEPs front MCP servers — useful pattern for opsbench when customers demand human-in-the-loop approval gates beyond pure Cedar.
discovered.notes: Closed-source SaaS gateway, but Permit ships OSS middleware (permit-fastmcp). Use as design reference, not vendor lock-in.

discovered.name: kyverno/kyverno
discovered.fit_reason: Kyverno CLI (`kyverno test`, `apply`, `json`, `validate`, `mutate`) is directly shellable by opsbench team-incident-response for AKS forensic playbooks — validate manifests offline, replay policies against captured snapshots, seal output as evidence.
discovered.notes: CLI is sufficient if the kyverno-mcp server is not desired. Output formats are LLM-friendly JSON.

discovered.name: open-policy-agent/opa
discovered.fit_reason: OPA core: `opa eval`, `opa test`, `opa exec`, `opa fmt`, `opa parse` are the standard tools any policy-aware DevOps agent must invoke. team-platform-engineering should ship a Cedar-gated MCP recipe wrapping these for inline policy decisions and replay.
discovered.notes: CNCF graduated. The bedrock of the OPA universe. Pair with Regal for linting and Conftest for IaC tests.

discovered.name: clawdreyhepburn/carapace
discovered.fit_reason: OpenClaw plugin that gates MCP tools, shell commands, and API calls via immutable Cedar policies — three-resource-type model (mcp.tool, shell.command, api.call) is exactly the partition opsbench needs for per-agent allowlists.
discovered.notes: Tiny but well-scoped. Borrow the three-resource Cedar schema.

discovered.name: agentralabs/agentic-contract
discovered.fit_reason: Rust-core policy engine with MCP server exposing 22 governance tools (policies, risk limits, approval gates, obligations, violations) persisted in a single .acon file with BLAKE3 checksums. The sealed single-file evidence pattern parallels opsbench's SHA-256 evidence design.
discovered.notes: Some of the advanced 'omniscience/precognition' tools look marketing-heavy; the core 22 are reasonable.

trends: Cedar is decisively winning the agent-authorization layer in 2026 (cedar-for-agents, AWS Bedrock AgentCore Policy GA in March 2026, ScopeBlind, Vectimus, Carapace, Sondera all converged on Cedar), while OPA/Rego retains dominance for K8s admission and IaC testing via Gatekeeper, Kyverno, Conftest, and Regal. A new "MCP gateway" category has emerged (ToolHive, ContextForge, ScopeBlind, Permit MCP Gateway) that sits between agents and MCP servers and enforces per-tool policies — many sign decisions cryptographically as offline-verifiable receipts, the same evidence-sealing pattern opsbench already implements. Vendor-published MCP servers from policy-engine maintainers (Nirmata's kyverno-mcp, official cedar-analysis-mcp-server) are the clearest signal that this space is maturing fast.
gaps: No mature MCP server yet wraps OPA/Gatekeeper or Conftest (only an auto-generated AG2 REST shim with 1 star) — opsbench could ship a first-class team-platform-engineering MCP recipe wrapping `opa eval`/`conftest test`/`gator` with Cedar gating and SHA-256 evidence sealing, immediately filling that void. Cedar-policy/cedar-for-agents provides a schema generator but no one has yet built an opinionated "incident-response Cedar policy pack" for AKS/K8s forensic workflows (Vectimus has a generic pack, not domain-specific). Finally, nothing bridges admission-controller findings (Kyverno/Gatekeeper violations) back into Cedar policy refinements for the next agent loop — an obvious opsbench differentiator: a feedback loop where K8s violations auto-tighten the agent's own Cedar allowlist.

discovered.name: hashicorp/vault-mcp-server
discovered.fit_reason: Official HashiCorp MCP server exposing Vault KV/PKI/mount management to AI agents over stdio + HTTP. Lets opsbench team-incident-response read short-lived credentials and rotate PKI during forensic workflows without hard-coding tokens.
discovered.notes: Beta — vendor explicitly recommends local-only deployment. Go binary, ships official Claude/VS Code/Gemini configs. Pairs well with Cedar gating since each tool maps to a Vault policy path.

discovered.name: hashicorp/hcp-vault-radar-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Vault Radar MCP exposes query_vault_radar_data_sources / _resources / _events / list_secret_types so an opsbench security agent can interrogate leaked-secret findings in natural language and trigger remediation playbooks during an incident.
discovered.notes: Vendor-hosted (also on AWS Marketplace). Read-only — does not return the secret value itself, only metadata, which fits opsbench's evidence-sealing model. Requires HCP account.

discovered.name: external-secrets/external-secrets
discovered.fit_reason: De-facto K8s operator that syncs from Vault/AWS SM/GCP SM/Azure KV/Akeyless/Pulumi ESC/CyberArk into native Secrets. opsbench can ship an ESO triage skill: read ExternalSecret CRs, diagnose SecretStore auth failures, propose ClusterSecretStore policy edits.
discovered.notes: Repo already contains a CLAUDE.md and is listed under GitHub's MCP Registry — strong upstream signal. Wrap kubectl get externalsecrets + Cedar policy gating; pairs cleanly with the existing AKS incident-response recipe.

discovered.name: Infisical/infisical-mcp-server
discovered.fit_reason: Official Infisical MCP exposes create/read/update/delete-secret, list-projects, invite-members. Universal Auth (Machine Identity client-id/secret) avoids personal creds — drop-in for opsbench team-platform-engineering when self-hosted Vault is overkill.
discovered.notes: Infisical itself supports dynamic secrets (rare among OSS managers). Self-hostable so it fits opsbench's air-gapped enterprise pitch.

discovered.name: DopplerHQ/mcp-server
discovered.fit_reason: Auto-generates MCP tools from Doppler's OpenAPI spec with read-only / project-scoped / config-scoped modes — the scope flags map directly to opsbench's per-agent tool allowlist concept and are a useful blueprint for scoped tool generation.
discovered.notes: Low stars but officially maintained. SaaS-only backend — include as optional recipe, not core dependency. Scope-flag pattern is worth porting even if Doppler itself isn't used.

discovered.name: bitwarden/mcp-server
discovered.fit_reason: Official Bitwarden MCP combines CLI (vault items, TOTP, Send) and API (collections, members, audit logs, policies). Useful for opsbench platform-engineering teams that store break-glass creds in Bitwarden Org rather than Vault.
discovered.notes: GPL-3.0 license — fine for an external MCP dep but cannot be vendored into opsbench (MIT/Apache). Vendor explicitly warns: local-only, never host publicly.

discovered.name: getsops/sops
discovered.fit_reason: CNCF sandbox project — the standard for encrypted-in-git secrets (YAML/JSON/ENV/INI) with AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure KV, age, PGP backends. opsbench can wrap sops via CLI-Anything for a GitOps-secret rotate/decrypt-in-incident skill.
discovered.notes: Now under getsops/ (transferred from mozilla/sops). Native Flux integration — perfect pair with opsbench's K8s incident workflows. No upstream MCP yet — wrap or write one.

discovered.name: FiloSottile/age
discovered.fit_reason: Modern small-footprint encryption tool — the recommended SOPS backend for homelab/SMB and increasingly enterprise. opsbench can ship an `age-keygen` + `age -d` helper skill for unsealing evidence bundles or rotating GitOps keys during IR.
discovered.notes: v1.3.1 adds post-quantum hybrid keys. Pure CLI — no API/MCP. Useful primitive, not a recipe on its own.

discovered.name: bitnami-labs/sealed-secrets
discovered.fit_reason: Kubeseal + controller is the dominant K8s-native sealed-secret pattern. opsbench team-incident-response needs a skill that can `kubeseal --re-encrypt` after key rotation and diff SealedSecret manifests during a breach — natural follow-up to existing K8s forensic recipes.
discovered.notes: v0.37.0 May 2026, still actively maintained even though some teams are migrating to SOPS+Flux. Cluster-scoped — limits what opsbench can do without Kubeconfig access, which actually plays well with Cedar gating.

discovered.name: trufflesecurity/trufflehog
discovered.fit_reason: 800+ secret types with live credential verification across git/GitHub/GitLab/S3/GCS/Docker/Jenkins/Postman/HF. Critical IR primitive: when opsbench investigates a breach, an agent skill that runs trufflehog + verifies active creds is table-stakes. Repo already ships .claude/skills, .codex/skills, .cursor/skills dirs.
discovered.notes: AGPL — fine to shell out to via CLI, must NOT vendor source. Has a Kubernetes Operator variant if opsbench wants continuous scanning instead of on-demand. Upstream Claude skills are a port-as-is candidate.

discovered.name: gitleaks/gitleaks
discovered.fit_reason: Fastest pre-commit/CI secret scanner with SARIF output. opsbench platform-engineering team can ship a skill that runs gitleaks against a repo + emits SHA-256-sealed evidence reports, matching the existing forensic evidence pattern.
discovered.notes: Maintainer is shifting focus to Betterleaks (security-patches-only mode). Still the de-facto default. Pairs well with trufflehog: gitleaks for speed/pre-commit, trufflehog for verification/IR.

discovered.name: sops-age-secret-management (mcpmarket skill)
discovered.fit_reason: Existing Claude Code skill that walks a user through SOPS+age bootstrap (key gen, .sops.yaml, .gitattributes, dependency install). Direct blueprint for opsbench's GitOps-secrets onboarding skill.
discovered.notes: Hosted on mcpmarket; not a repo. Treat as design reference for opsbench's own skill rather than a dep.

discovered.name: mohshomis/AIVault
discovered.fit_reason: Interesting pattern: AI references secrets by symbolic name ($GITHUB_TOKEN), MCP server injects values at exec time, scrubs leaked values from output, returns clean result. Directly applicable to opsbench's Cedar-gated agent model — the scrub-on-egress idea is novel.
discovered.notes: Tiny project but the inject-at-runtime + egress-scrub pattern is worth copying into opsbench's tool allowlist layer rather than depending on the repo.

discovered.name: Keeper-Security/keeper-mcp-golang-docker
discovered.fit_reason: Official Keeper Secrets Manager MCP, Dockerized. Useful only for enterprise teams already using Keeper — link as a cross-reference so opsbench users on Keeper know it exists.
discovered.notes: Niche customer base. Not a default opsbench recipe.

discovered.name: gecochief/id.wispera
discovered.fit_reason: "Passport & visa" system for AI agent credentials — encrypted vault, policy engine, audit trails, delegation chains, drop-in MCP replacement for plaintext credential storage across MCP/OpenAI/Anthropic/LangChain. Architectural blueprint for how opsbench might govern downstream MCP creds.
discovered.notes: Very low stars, early-stage. Worth reading for the delegation-chain + policy-engine pattern, not yet a dep.

discovered.name: external-secrets/kubernetes-external-secrets
discovered.fit_reason: Reference architecture pages on external-secrets.io document the SecretStore / ClusterSecretStore / ExternalSecret / PushSecret CRD model. Direct input for opsbench's threat-model docs and Cedar policy templates for secrets-mgmt namespace.
discovered.notes: Docs site only — pair with the operator repo above. No code to vendor.

trends: In 2026, every major secrets-management vendor ships an official MCP server (HashiCorp Vault + Vault Radar, Infisical, Doppler, Bitwarden, Keeper) and HashiCorp is bundling Vault MCP with Terraform MCP for end-to-end "agentic IaC + secrets" workflows on AWS Marketplace. The GitOps stack is consolidating on SOPS + age (Flux-native) over Sealed Secrets for new deployments, while External Secrets Operator has become the universal multi-cloud sync layer. Secret-scanning is bifurcating into two complementary roles: gitleaks (fast, regex, pre-commit) and trufflehog (slow, verified-active, IR/CI) — and trufflehog now ships native .claude/.codex/.cursor skill folders, signaling that AI-agent integration is the new must-have for security CLIs.
gaps: There is no opinionated "incident-response for leaked secrets" skill that chains scan → verify-active → rotate-via-Vault → seal evidence → file ticket — opsbench could own this category by combining trufflehog + Vault MCP + its existing SHA-256 evidence sealing. Cedar-gated, scoped MCP credential proxies (the AIVault / Wispera pattern: inject-by-name + egress-scrub + per-tool allowlist) are nascent toys today, but match opsbench's threat model exactly and could become a flagship recipe. Finally, nobody ships a unified "SOPS / Sealed-Secrets / ESO triage" skill that a platform-engineering agent can use to diagnose a stuck ExternalSecret across all three backends — clear whitespace for opsbench team-platform-engineering.

discovered.name: awslabs/mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Official AWS-supported monorepo of 30+ MCP servers (EKS, Cost Explorer, Pricing, Billing, CloudWatch, IAM, S3, Lambda, CDK). The EKS MCP server in particular gives opsbench's team-incident-response agents native EKS cluster introspection, log retrieval, and troubleshooting workflows that mirror the existing AKS forensic recipes.
discovered.notes: 9k+ stars, actively pushed daily. AWS now recommends the 'Agent Toolkit for AWS' as the successor for production use, but the EKS/Cost/Pricing servers remain canonical. Per-server scoping is essential — wire each server behind a Cedar policy and per-agent allowlist to stop blast-radius creep.

discovered.name: aws/agent-toolkit-for-aws
discovered.fit_reason: AWS's official, production-grade successor to awslabs/mcp — bundles MCP servers, skills, and plugins for AI agents to build on AWS. Direct template/blueprint for how opsbench should ship its own team-platform-engineering skills (allowlist + Cedar + evidence patterns map cleanly).
discovered.notes: Newer than awslabs/mcp (early 2026) but officially blessed. Worth studying its skill manifest format before opsbench locks its own.

discovered.name: microsoft/mcp (Azure MCP Server)
discovered.fit_reason: Catalog of official Microsoft MCP server implementations including the Azure MCP Server, which covers AKS, Container Apps, Cosmos, AI Foundry, KeyVault, Monitor, Storage and more via natural language. opsbench already targets AKS forensic incident response — adopting this server directly extends every existing recipe with first-party Azure tool calls.
discovered.notes: Ships built into VS 2026 and surfaced via the plugin:azure MCP catalog already loaded in this session. Auth is via az CLI / managed identity; opsbench evidence sealing should capture the subscription/tenant context on every call.

discovered.name: googleapis/gcloud-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Official Google-maintained MCP server that wraps the gcloud CLI for natural-language GCP operations. Lets opsbench add GCP/GKE parity to its existing AKS+EKS team-incident-response coverage without writing a new client; gcloud's project/IAM model maps cleanly to per-agent Cedar gating.
discovered.notes: Official googleapis org. Auth via ADC; needs --impersonate-service-account discipline. Pair with GCP audit log capture for evidence sealing.

discovered.name: Cloudflare official MCP servers (mcp-server-cloudflare + Code Mode)
discovered.fit_reason: Cloudflare publishes a suite of managed remote MCP servers (Workers, R2, D1, DNS, observability, builds) plus a new Code Mode server that compresses 2,500+ API endpoints into a single tool surface. High-value for an opsbench team-network-engineering or edge-incident skill — DNS analytics + observability cover the network forensic gap.
discovered.notes: Remote OAuth flow is required; opsbench needs an OAuth client handler. Code Mode reduces token bloat but executes generated JS inside Cloudflare's runtime — review trust boundary before allowlisting.

discovered.name: digitalocean/digitalocean-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Official DigitalOcean MCP server with remote endpoints live for Accounts, App Platform, Databases, DOKS, Droplets, Insights, Marketplace, Networking, and Spaces. Gives opsbench a fourth cloud (beyond AKS/EKS/GKE) covering the indie/SMB segment where many platform-engineering customers live.
discovered.notes: Remote MCP means no local binary; auth via DO PAT. DOKS endpoint pairs well with existing Kubernetes forensic recipes.

discovered.name: nityeshaga/hetzner-mcp-server
discovered.fit_reason: Community-built Hetzner Cloud MCP server explicitly aimed at Claude Code — covers server provisioning, SSH keys, server types, images, datacenters. Useful for EU-focused customers and for opsbench's team-platform-engineering as a reference for wrapping a flat REST API into MCP tools.
discovered.notes: Single-maintainer community project — vet for maintenance signal before depending on it. Could also be re-implemented as opsbench skill calling hcloud CLI directly via CLI-Anything pattern.

discovered.name: hashicorp/terraform-mcp-server
discovered.fit_reason: Official HashiCorp MCP server with 35+ tools spanning the Terraform registry, Stacks, policy sets, HCP workspaces/variables, and runs. Critical for opsbench team-platform-engineering — most cloud infra is provisioned via Terraform, so post-incident remediation flows need to read/modify state safely.
discovered.notes: MPL-2.0 (file-level copyleft) — fine to integrate, just don't vendor source into MIT/Apache opsbench files. Plugin:terraform is already loaded in this session. Gate apply_run/discard_run behind explicit human confirmation as the server itself recommends.

discovered.name: awslabs/iam-policy-autopilot
discovered.fit_reason: Static-analysis tool + MCP server for generating least-privilege AWS IAM policies from code. Direct fit for opsbench's security posture — after an incident, opsbench can auto-propose tightened IAM policies and seal them as evidence-bound remediation artifacts.
discovered.notes: AWS-only. Pairs naturally with Cedar gating since both are policy-as-code; opsbench could surface IAM diffs in the same evidence bundle.

discovered.name: prowler-cloud/prowler
discovered.fit_reason: Open-source cloud security platform (572+ AWS checks, 41 compliance frameworks, plus Azure/GCP/K8s) that now ships an official Claude Code plugin bundling a Prowler MCP server + 'framework-compliance-triage' skill. Drops directly into opsbench as a security audit skill for team-security-engineering with no glue code.
discovered.notes: Free for MCP knowledge base; commercial app features need API key. Plugin architecture exactly mirrors opsbench's skill+MCP pattern — strong reference for opsbench's own packaging.

discovered.name: k8sgpt-ai/k8sgpt
discovered.fit_reason: CNCF project that scans Kubernetes clusters, identifies problems, and ships an MCP server exposing those analyzers as agent tools with built-in PII anonymization. Slots cleanly into opsbench team-incident-response — k8sgpt does the triage, opsbench does the gated remediation.
discovered.notes: CNCF, mature. Anonymization layer is a built-in win for opsbench's evidence-sealing story — sensitive cluster strings never leave the boundary.

discovered.name: kagent-dev/kagent
discovered.fit_reason: CNCF Sandbox agentic AI framework that runs AI agents inside Kubernetes to automate ops, with an MCP server interfacing Istio/Helm/Argo/Prometheus/Grafana/Cilium. Useful as a reference architecture for opsbench's roadmap of running agents on-cluster (vs only as a coding assistant).
discovered.notes: Created by Solo.io 2025, still sandbox-stage. Stack overlap is more about ops mesh than cloud-provider APIs per se, but the MCP-server-per-component pattern is exactly what opsbench needs.

discovered.name: cloudquery/cloudquery (+ CloudQuery MCP Server)
discovered.fit_reason: Cloud asset inventory tool (70+ sources: AWS, Azure, GCP, K8s, Cloudflare, Okta, PagerDuty) with a new MCP server letting agents query a structured snapshot of every account. Perfect for cross-cloud forensic queries in opsbench team-incident-response — point-in-time data avoids slow API enumeration during a fire.
discovered.notes: MPL-2.0 — same caveat as Terraform MCP. Requires a backing Postgres/ClickHouse store; opsbench would need to document the deploy footprint.

discovered.name: turbot/steampipe (+ Steampipe MCP)
discovered.fit_reason: select * from cloud — SQL over live AWS/Azure/GCP/K8s/GitHub APIs via Postgres FDWs, now with an official MCP server. Complementary to CloudQuery (live vs snapshot); opsbench can offer both as alternative skills and let users pick latency vs. freshness tradeoff.
discovered.notes: AGPL — must NOT be vendored into opsbench; run as an external MCP server only. v2.3.6 as of Feb 2026.

discovered.name: robusta-dev/robusta + HolmesGPT
discovered.fit_reason: HolmesGPT is an agentic AI investigator that runs against Prometheus/Loki/Tempo/PagerDuty/OpsGenie + cloud providers, executing runbooks and correlating observability data. Strong template for opsbench's incident-response agent loop; many of its tool integrations could be lifted as opsbench MCP recipes.
discovered.notes: CNCF Sandbox via Robusta. Already production-deployed in many SRE shops, so its tool taxonomy is a credible north star for opsbench's team-incident-response skill catalogue.

discovered.name: kubeshop/botkube
discovered.fit_reason: Slack/Teams/Discord ChatOps bridge for Kubernetes with an AI assistant that can analyze alerts, suggest fixes, and run kubectl/helm. Useful as the *delivery* surface for opsbench incident output — opsbench produces evidence-sealed remediation; Botkube routes the approval flow into chat.
discovered.notes: Not an MCP server itself; integrate via cross-reference / webhook bridge. Slack plugin in this session can already drive much of this; Botkube is the K8s-aware variant worth documenting.

trends: Hyperscalers and major infra vendors have all shipped first-party MCP servers in 2025-2026 (AWS Labs' 30+ server monorepo, Microsoft Azure MCP built into VS 2026, googleapis/gcloud-mcp, Cloudflare's managed remote MCP suite + Code Mode, DigitalOcean's 9-service remote MCP, HashiCorp Terraform MCP, Prowler's Claude Code plugin) — MCP is now the de-facto agent-control plane for cloud ops. The pattern is converging on (1) a fat catalog server per vendor with per-service scoping, (2) remote/OAuth deployment over stdio, and (3) bundling skills + MCP server + policy together as a single Claude Code/Copilot plugin. CNCF-side, Kubernetes-specific agentic projects (k8sgpt, kagent, HolmesGPT) are graduating from "AI explains kubectl" to autonomous investigators with their own MCP surfaces.
gaps: Nobody is shipping a cross-cloud incident-response *opinionated* agent that ties policy gating (Cedar/OPA), per-tool allowlists, and cryptographic evidence sealing to the underlying MCP calls — every vendor MCP server gives you raw tool surface with no provenance or scope discipline, leaving the integrator to bolt that on. Hetzner/Scaleway/OVH/Linode coverage is anemic (mostly single-maintainer community MCPs), so a polished EU-sovereign cloud skill bundle is a niche opsbench could own. Finally, there's no good "forensic evidence bundle" standard across clouds — opsbench's SHA-256-sealed bundle pattern could become a reference format that the ecosystem adopts, especially if paired with a CLI to verify bundles offline.

discovered.name: velero-mcp (benzaidfoued)
discovered.fit_reason: Read-only MCP server exposing Velero Backup/Schedule CRDs to AI agents (Claude/Cursor/Copilot). Directly drop-in for an opsbench team-incident-response 'investigate-backup-state' recipe — agent can list backups/schedules and synthesize Velero YAML without touching the cluster (perfect for low-blast-radius restore investigations behind Cedar policy gating).
discovered.notes: Very low stars / Glama flagged low maintenance score, but functional and the only Velero-specific MCP that exists. Safe surface (read-only by design); pin commit if vendoring. Python 3.10 + MCP SDK + k8s client.

discovered.name: restic-mcp (mohsenil85)
discovered.fit_reason: MCP wrapper around restic CLI exposing list_snapshots, browse, diff, restore, check, prune, forget — the full DR verify/restore loop. opsbench team-incident-response can call this to validate backup integrity post-incident; matches the SHA-256 evidence-sealing model since restic itself is content-addressed.
discovered.notes: Tiny project but covers both read and write restic ops via --json. Write tools (restore/prune/forget) MUST be gated by Cedar policy in opsbench. Thin async wrapper; easy to fork/harden.

discovered.name: velero-io/velero
discovered.fit_reason: The canonical Kubernetes backup CLI/operator. opsbench team-incident-response and a future team-platform-engineering skill should shell out to `velero backup`, `velero restore`, `velero describe` for AKS DR runbooks. CNCF-sandbox-bound after Broadcom's 2026 donation makes it the safe long-term bet.
discovered.notes: No first-party MCP yet — wrap via HKUDS/CLI-Anything pattern or fork velero-mcp. JSON output via `--output json` available on most commands. RBAC scoping must be enforced per agent allowlist.

discovered.name: kanisterio/kanister
discovered.fit_reason: CNCF-sandbox data-protection workflow operator with Blueprint/ActionSet/Profile CRDs — application-aware backup hooks for stateful workloads (Postgres, Mongo, Cassandra). opsbench can ship a skill that authors Blueprints + triggers ActionSets, closing the app-consistent DR gap Velero alone can't cover.
discovered.notes: Originally built by Kasten/Veeam team; pairs naturally with Kopia. No MCP server — pattern to copy into an opsbench skill that emits Blueprint YAML.

discovered.name: k8up-io/k8up
discovered.fit_reason: Production Kubernetes backup operator (BSI 3-clause, OpenShift-compatible) with Backup/Restore/Schedule/PrePostExec CRDs wrapping restic. Strong reference for opsbench's own backup recipe schema and Prometheus integration (matches the metrics/alerting surface opsbench wants).
discovered.notes: Helm-distributed, written in Go, production since 2019. No MCP. Useful as a CRD pattern blueprint and as a shell-out target (`kubectl apply` of Backup CR).

discovered.name: stashed/stash (KubeStash)
discovered.fit_reason: AppsCode's Kubernetes-native backup operator (BackupConfiguration, Repository, BackupSession CRDs) using restic + CSI VolumeSnapshotter. Mature CRD model that an opsbench team-platform-engineering 'backup-policy-author' skill can imitate for multi-tenant/multi-database flows.
discovered.notes: Activity slowing in favor of successor KubeStash (kubestash.com). Enterprise edition behind paywall for DB addons — community CRD set is still useful blueprint material.

discovered.name: garethgeorge/backrest
discovered.fit_reason: Go-based web UI + cron orchestrator over restic with plan/schedule/retention abstraction. opsbench can lift the plan-schema pattern and Slack/Discord/Gotify webhook design into a 'host-level backup' recipe for non-K8s nodes where Velero doesn't apply.
discovered.notes: GPL-3.0 — cannot vendor source into opsbench's permissive codebase, but pattern/copy is fine. No MCP, no API exposed externally.

discovered.name: Runbook-Agent/RunbookAI
discovered.fit_reason: AI-powered incident response agent with hypothesis-driven investigation and approval-gated remediation — the exact 'approve-then-execute' model opsbench's Cedar policy gate already implements. Strong cross-reference for backup-DR runbook execution patterns.
discovered.notes: Not backup-specific but architectural fit is excellent: runbook-aware context + approval gating maps 1:1 to opsbench's tool allowlists and evidence-sealing. Confirm license before borrowing code.

discovered.name: seriohub/vui-ui (Velero UI)
discovered.fit_reason: Multi-cluster Velero dashboard with cron heatmap, notifications, log streaming. Useful reference for an opsbench operator-facing 'backup status digest' artifact (markdown rather than HTML) summarizing Velero state across clusters during incidents.
discovered.notes: UI-only; no agent surface. Borrow the data-shape and per-cluster aggregation logic for opsbench output formatting.

discovered.name: etcd Claude Code skill (claudskills.com)
discovered.fit_reason: Community Claude Code skill covering etcd backup/restore (snapshot save/restore), cluster setup, TLS, monitoring — the control-plane half of K8s DR that opsbench team-incident-response needs alongside Velero (PV/workload) and Longhorn (CSI). Drops into ~/.claude/skills/etcd/.
discovered.notes: Hosted on claudskills.com; mirror to opsbench skills tree and align with Cedar policy. License/attribution must be verified on the SKILL.md upstream.

discovered.name: longhorn/longhorn
discovered.fit_reason: CNCF distributed block storage with native RecurringJob CRDs for scheduled snapshots, S3/NFS backup targets, and cross-cluster DR volumes. opsbench skill can author RecurringJob + BackupTarget specs and shell out to `kubectl -n longhorn-system` for failover orchestration.
discovered.notes: No dedicated MCP. Wrap via CLI-Anything or write a thin MCP exposing list_backups/list_recurring_jobs/trigger_dr_failover. DR-volume failover is destructive — must be Cedar-gated.

discovered.name: kopia/kopia
discovered.fit_reason: Fast cross-platform encrypted backup CLI/server with content-addressed storage, policy-based scheduling, S3/B2/Azure/GCS backends. Pairs with Kanister and is the modern restic alternative for opsbench host-level / DB-dump DR recipes.
discovered.notes: No MCP server exists yet — gap opsbench could fill. `kopia server` exposes a REST API which is easier to wrap than restic's CLI-only surface.

trends: Backup/DR is shifting from static cron + UI tools to declarative Kubernetes operators (Velero, Kanister, K8up, Stash) whose CRDs are agent-friendly — and Broadcom's 2026 donation of Velero to CNCF accelerates that. The MCP layer is just emerging: a handful of single-author MCP servers (velero-mcp, restic-mcp) exist but are tiny and read-mostly; meanwhile community 'agentic runbook' patterns (Cast AI, RunbookAI) are normalizing approval-gated remediation that matches opsbench's Cedar/evidence model. Restic remains the de-facto data plane under nearly every operator (K8up, Stash, Kanister-via-Kopia, Backrest).
gaps: No production-grade MCP server exists for Velero, Longhorn, Kanister, Kopia, or etcd snapshots — the existing servers have under 5 stars and are single-author. There is no agent-callable 'cross-layer DR' tool that composes etcd + Velero + Longhorn + Kanister into a single restore plan with evidence-sealed transitions, which is exactly the gap an opsbench team-incident-response 'kubernetes-dr-recovery' recipe can fill. A second clear gap: no skill exists today that authors Cedar-style policy alongside backup CRDs (who-can-restore-what-from-when), which opsbench could ship as the canonical DR-policy-as-code blueprint.

discovered.name: nomagicln/mcp-harbor
discovered.fit_reason: Direct MCP wrapper over Harbor 2.x API (list/get/create/delete projects, repositories, tags, Helm charts). Drop-in surface for a future opsbench team-platform-engineering or supply-chain triage skill needing registry inspection during incident response.
discovered.notes: TypeScript/Node. Last commit ~14 months old as of 2026-06; light maintenance but functional. No write-side gating, so combine with Cedar-policy allowlist before exposing delete_* tools.

discovered.name: mshegolev/harbor-registry-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Fresher (May 2026) alternative Harbor MCP, adds storage-report and cleanup-candidate tools that map directly to incident-response disk-pressure or image-bloat scenarios. Useful when an AKS node is wedged on registry pull failures.
discovered.notes: Zero stars but recently active; treat as 'evaluation candidate', not battle-tested. Same vendor coverage as nomagicln/mcp-harbor with newer scope.

discovered.name: quay/quay-mcp-server
discovered.fit_reason: Official Red Hat Quay MCP server, auto-derives tools from OpenAPI for manifests/orgs/repos/robots/tags. Required if opsbench supports OpenShift / Quay shops alongside Harbor for parity in registry recipes.
discovered.notes: Stale-looking (~12 months) but vendor-blessed (quay org). OAuth-based auth; review token scopes before allowlisting write tools.

discovered.name: shizhMSFT/oras-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Multi-registry OCI inspection MCP (image platform metadata, tag/version search across MCR/GHCR, signature-type detection). Perfect for an opsbench supply-chain forensic skill that has to inspect signatures across ECR/ACR/GHCR/Harbor uniformly.
discovered.notes: Microsoft-employee maintained, built on oras-go (no CLI dep). Low stars; verify maturity before relying on signature detection in evidence pipelines.

discovered.name: awslabs/mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Official AWS MCP suite includes Finch (build/push to ECR) and ECS MCP servers that expose ECR resources; the AWS API MCP can read ECR repos, images, lifecycle policies, and scan findings. Lets opsbench reach ECR without writing custom AWS recipes.
discovered.notes: Very active, ECR is not a single dedicated server but exposed via AWS API / ECS / Finch servers; allowlist carefully (IAM scope creep risk).

discovered.name: sigstore/cosign
discovered.fit_reason: De facto image signing/verification CLI; opsbench evidence-sealing pipeline could cosign-sign forensic bundles, and a registry forensic skill needs `cosign verify` / `cosign tree` to attest pulled images against Rekor.
discovered.notes: Mature, keyless OIDC mode integrates cleanly with GitHub Actions identity. Wrap via CLI-Anything; no upstream MCP from sigstore project itself.

discovered.name: CSOAI-ORG/sigstore-cosign-mcp
discovered.fit_reason: Existing MCP wrapping cosign + Rekor transparency-log verification, saves writing a CLI-Anything wrapper if its tool surface matches opsbench needs. Useful in supply-chain incident skills that must verify signatures during an investigation.
discovered.notes: Zero stars, single-author; audit before trusting in evidence path. Could be vendored as a smaller template-blueprint if upstream stalls.

discovered.name: aquasecurity/trivy
discovered.fit_reason: The dominant container/IaC/SBOM scanner. An opsbench registry-incident skill must run `trivy image` against suspicious tags during forensic triage and emit SARIF/CycloneDX into the sealed evidence bundle.
discovered.notes: Aqua also publishes an official Trivy MCP server (separate repo) — consider that for direct mcp-recipe instead of pure CLI wrap if first-class MCP is preferred.

discovered.name: anchore/grype
discovered.fit_reason: Second-source CVE scanner for cross-checking Trivy findings; consumes Syft SBOMs and emits SBOM/VEX. Useful when opsbench needs corroborated CVE evidence (single-scanner blind spots are a common audit finding).
discovered.notes: v0.111 added CSAF VEX transformer (Apr 2026). Pair with Syft below for SBOM-first workflows.

discovered.name: anchore/syft
discovered.fit_reason: SBOM generator (SPDX, CycloneDX) for container images, filesystems, and source. opsbench evidence bundles should include a sealed SBOM for every implicated image; Syft is the de facto generator.
discovered.notes: Apache-2.0, Go binary, scriptable. Output formats align with the SHA-256 evidence-sealing pattern opsbench already uses.

discovered.name: containers/skopeo
discovered.fit_reason: Daemon-less registry CLI for inspect/copy/delete across registries — exactly what an air-gapped incident-response sandbox needs to pull a suspect image into evidence storage without standing up containerd. Used by Red Hat and most CI/CD pipelines.
discovered.notes: Daemonless = trivially sandboxable for forensic copies. License Apache-2.0; bundle as a sealed-binary helper in the team-incident-response skill.

discovered.name: google/go-containerregistry (crane)
discovered.fit_reason: `crane` CLI: copy/append/mutate/config/manifest on remote images without Docker. Faster than skopeo for read-only metadata queries; ideal helper inside a per-agent allowlist for read-only registry forensic agents.
discovered.notes: Three CLIs (crane/gcrane/krane) share build; krane variant works in-cluster with K8s auth — useful inside the existing AKS recipes.

discovered.name: oras-project/oras
discovered.fit_reason: OCI Artifact CLI (push/pull arbitrary blobs, attached referrers). opsbench could push sealed evidence bundles as OCI artifacts into Harbor/GHCR with cosign signatures — gives auditors a registry-native chain of custody.
discovered.notes: Pairs with shizhMSFT/oras-mcp for an agent-callable surface. Apache-2.0.

discovered.name: goharbor/harbor-cli
discovered.fit_reason: Vendor-official Harbor CLI; richer than the MCP wrappers for admin ops (replication, retention rules, robot accounts). Use as the underlying CLI for any cli-anything-wrap until a stable vendor MCP exists.
discovered.notes: Active, official goharbor maintainer. Smaller star count is normal for vendor CLIs.

discovered.name: goharbor/terraform-provider-harbor
discovered.fit_reason: Pattern/blueprint for declaring Harbor projects, robot accounts, retention rules, replication policies as code. opsbench team-platform-engineering skills can lean on it for GitOps-style registry config rather than imperative MCP calls.
discovered.notes: MIT-licensed, modules can be referenced in Cedar-gated change skills.

discovered.name: distribution/distribution

trends: Container-registry tooling is converging on three layers that all surface to agents: (1) vendor-published MCP servers (Harbor by community, Quay official, AWS official suite covering ECR via Finch/ECS) wrapping registry REST/OAuth APIs, (2) the cosign/sigstore + Rekor stack becoming the de facto signature substrate that Kyverno-style admission controllers and Trivy/Grype evidence pipelines depend on, and (3) OCI Artifacts (via ORAS) emerging as the carrier format for non-image payloads like SBOMs, attestations, and now agent skills/MCP packages themselves. Multi-scanner cross-checking (Trivy + Grype with Syft SBOMs as the shared substrate, CSAF VEX in Apr 2026) is now standard practice for high-assurance supply-chain workflows.
gaps: There is no opinionated, Cedar-gated multi-registry forensic MCP recipe that unifies Harbor + Quay + ECR + ACR + GHCR behind a single signed-evidence-bundle output — every existing MCP server is single-vendor and most expose write tools without policy gating, which is exactly the niche opsbench's per-agent allowlist + SHA-256 sealing pattern can fill. There is also no agent-facing "registry incident replay" skill that combines skopeo/crane to copy a suspect image into a sealed lab, Syft+Grype+Trivy to triple-attest the SBOM/CVE state, and cosign verify to assert the signature chain — opsbench could ship that as the canonical team-incident-response recipe. Finally, the official Harbor org has no first-party MCP server, so a goharbor-blessed opsbench skill that wraps harbor-cli via CLI-Anything would be a high-leverage upstream contribution.

trends: CI/CD MCP servers are consolidating around official vendor implementations: GitHub, Buildkite, Argo (CD and Workflows), Tekton, and Harness all now ship first-party MCP or skill bundles, displacing the wave of single-author servers from 2025. Two new shapes are emerging in 2026 — (1) "skill bundles" (Flux, Harness) shipped as Markdown directly to Claude Code/Codex/Cursor without needing custom MCP code, and (2) governance MCP servers (Delimit, AgentShield, Kryptonclaw, Open Code Review) that gate AI-written code with deterministic breaking-change detection and SARIF-emitting security scans. GitOps + AI ("agentic GitOps") is the most active narrative, with both Flux and Argo investing in agent-driven sync, debug and remediation paths.
gaps: No project today combines Cedar-policy gating + per-agent tool allowlists + cryptographic evidence sealing across both Kubernetes state and CI/CD pipeline state — opsbench can own this niche by stitching the existing kube MCP surface together with github-mcp-server / mcp-for-argocd / Tekton MCP under one signed allowlist, sealing the resulting workflow logs and ArgoCD sync events into the same evidence chain. There is also no canonical multi-CI incident-response skill (one that triages a failed deploy across Actions, GitLab CI, Tekton, and Argo without knowing the platform a priori); the Flux and Harness bundles are vendor-specific. Finally, opsbench could ship the first standardized "CI/CD forensic" recipe — a Kryptonclaw-style supply-chain audit fused with workflow-run log capture and Argo sync history, gated by Cedar and tied to the existing SHA-256 evidence pipeline.

trends: Identity vendors are racing to ship first-party MCP servers in 2026 -- Okta, Authentik (via official blog endorsement) and Casdoor (which now markets itself as 'agent-first IAM') all have native or sanctioned MCP surfaces, and the broader pattern is to expose admin APIs as MCP tools guarded by short-lived OAuth2 device-flow or Private-Key-JWT tokens. A parallel trend is OAuth-2.1-gated MCP gateways (agentic-community/mcp-gateway-registry, babs/mcp-auth-proxy, mcp-auth/python+js) that sit in front of arbitrary MCP servers and federate identity from existing IdPs, which dovetails with calls in MarkTechPost's 'Best Authentication Platforms for AI Agents and MCP Servers in 2026' roundup. On the kubectl side, OIDC plugins (kubelogin) and Pomerium-style identity-aware proxies are being repositioned as 'agentic access gateways' that bind agent invocations to verifiable human or service identities.
gaps: There is no opsbench-style skill that unifies these MCP servers behind a single 'IAM forensic' team agent -- i.e. a recipe where one incident-response agent can pivot across Keycloak/Okta/Authentik/Ory in the same investigation, correlate sessions with the k8s audit log, and emit SHA-256-sealed evidence of the access path. Ory Kratos and Dex have only thin community MCP wrappers (one is a one-star, license-less repo; Dex has none at all), so a vendor-quality 'opsbench-skill-iam' covering Dex + Hydra + Kratos would fill a real hole. Finally, no project yet ties OIDC token issuance to opsbench's Cedar policy gating + evidence sealing for agent tool calls -- that synthesis (IdP-issued JWT -> Cedar policy decision -> sealed audit record) is the natural place for opsbench to lead.

trends: The cybersec-soc ecosystem is rapidly standardizing on MCP as the glue between Claude Code/Cursor and the open SOC stack (Wazuh, TheHive/Cortex, MISP, Velociraptor, OpenCTI, Suricata/Zeek), with multiple competing implementations per tool already mature in mid-2026. Composability is the headline pattern: solomonneas's 7-MCP suite, FunnyWolf's agentic-soc-platform, and SOCFortress are converging on the same Wazuh -> Zeek/Suricata -> MISP/OpenCTI -> ATT&CK -> TheHive/IRIS -> Cortex pivot chain. A parallel emerging trend is "Sigma for agents": Agent Threat Rules and sigma-ai are bringing detection-as-code to agent runtime threats (prompt injection, tool poisoning, MCP attacks).
gaps: Despite the MCP boom, almost no project wires Cedar-style policy gating or cryptographic evidence sealing into the SOC MCP calls - opsbench's SHA-256 evidence + per-agent allowlist model is genuinely differentiated and could become the reference safety layer for SOC MCP composition. There is also no opinionated "team-soc-analyst" skill bundle that orchestrates the existing single-tool MCPs into a single Cedar-policed investigation loop with auditable transcripts, leaving room for opsbench to publish a curated, allowlist-shaped recipe pack. Finally, detection-engineering (Sigma authoring, ATT&CK coverage diffing) and agent-self-monitoring via Agent Threat Rules are under-served and would extend opsbench's IR posture into preventative detection.

discovered.name: k8sgpt-ai/k8sgpt
discovered.fit_reason: CNCF Sandbox tool that diagnoses K8s/AKS clusters and now ships an embedded MCP server (port 8089) exposing analyze/list/logs/events tools - opsbench can wire it as the read-only triage layer for team-incident-response before any forensic-seal step.
discovered.notes: Read-only by default which fits opsbench's allowlist model. New analyzers in 2026 cover storage, PSP, RBAC - aligns with security forensics. Multi-LLM backend (OpenAI, Bedrock, Gemini, Ollama) helps avoid vendor lock.

discovered.name: containers/kubernetes-mcp-server
discovered.fit_reason: Red Hat/containers org MCP server covering Kubernetes and OpenShift CRUD plus events and logs; would give opsbench's AKS forensic recipes a vendor-neutral fallback when running outside Azure-MCP context and simplifies allowlist auditing because every tool is one Go binary.
discovered.notes: Active daily commits; suitable for vendoring as a sidecar container next to the agent. Cedar policy must restrict mutating verbs.

discovered.name: kagent-dev/kagent
discovered.fit_reason: CNCF Sandbox K8s-native agent framework with bundled ToolServer CRDs for Istio/Helm/Argo/Prometheus/Grafana/Cilium - a strong blueprint for opsbench's planned team-platform-engineering skills and a model for declaring per-agent tool allowlists as Kubernetes resources.
discovered.notes: Heavier than opsbench needs (it deploys a controller); use as architectural reference for tool-server registry pattern rather than direct dependency.

discovered.name: kagent-dev/kmcp
discovered.fit_reason: CLI + K8s controller that scaffolds, tests, and deploys MCP servers - opsbench can adopt kmcp as the local devloop for shipping its own custom forensic MCP servers (evidence sealer, Cedar-policy gate, allowlist verifier) instead of hand-rolling.
discovered.notes: Pairs naturally with kagent but works standalone. Active 2026 development.

discovered.name: grafana/mcp-grafana
discovered.fit_reason: Official Grafana MCP server exposing dashboards, Loki, Tempo, Sift, Incident, and alert rules read-only - lets opsbench's incident-response agent pull metric/log windows around the forensic timestamp without writing Prometheus scrapers.
discovered.notes: Vendor-supported; OAuth or service-account token. Compose with giantswarm/mcp-prometheus when no Grafana is present.

discovered.name: giantswarm/mcp-prometheus
discovered.fit_reason: Go MCP server exposing 18 read-only Prometheus/Mimir HTTP API tools (PromQL instant+range, label discovery, alerting rules, TSDB stats) - direct fit for opsbench's evidence-collection phase when teams run bare Prometheus without Grafana.
discovered.notes: Smaller community but Giant Swarm is a reputable K8s vendor; read-only surface aligns well with opsbench's evidence-only Cedar policies.

discovered.name: argoproj-labs/mcp-for-argocd
discovered.fit_reason: Argo project labs MCP server for ArgoCD - critical for opsbench's planned team-platform-engineering recipes that need GitOps visibility (out-of-sync apps, last-synced revision) when correlating a deploy with an incident.
discovered.notes: Argo project incubated which signals long-term support. Use read-only token; restrict app sync via Cedar.

discovered.name: PagerDuty/pagerduty-mcp-server
discovered.fit_reason: Official PagerDuty MCP server plus the new PagerDuty Claude Code plugin in Anthropic's marketplace - lets opsbench tag forensic evidence with the originating PD incident ID and pull on-call/service ownership during triage.
discovered.notes: Companion repo PagerDuty/claude-code-plugins ships /risk-score skill - worth cross-referencing as opsbench skill pattern.

discovered.name: rootly-ai-labs (Rootly MCP Server)
discovered.fit_reason: Vendor MCP server for Rootly incident lifecycle (timeline, retros, action items) - useful for shops on Rootly; opsbench could expose it as an optional plugin to attach evidence bundles to a Rootly incident.
discovered.notes: Closed-source SaaS dependency, but the MCP server itself is open and the API surface is public. Less universal than PagerDuty.

discovered.name: GoogleCloudPlatform/kubectl-ai
discovered.fit_reason: Google's natural-language kubectl wrapper with multi-LLM and MCP endpoint support - opsbench can shell out to it for plain-English query translation in team-incident-response when freeform questions arrive, then verify the suggested kubectl against allowlist.
discovered.notes: Suggests-then-runs design needs guardrails; wire via cli-anything with --dry-run first.

discovered.name: headlamp-k8s/plugins (ai-assistant)
discovered.fit_reason: Headlamp (Kubernetes SIG UI) AI Assistant plugin source - opsbench can copy its MCP wiring patterns and prompt scaffolding for the multi-cluster Kubernetes context-switching that team-platform-engineering will need.
discovered.notes: Useful for UI/UX patterns; Headlamp v0.41 ships MCP-backed assistant natively. Reference rather than dependency.

discovered.name: anthropics/claude-cookbooks (SRE agent + sre_mcp_server.py)
discovered.fit_reason: Anthropic's own canonical SRE incident responder pattern with a runnable MCP server stub for PagerDuty + Confluence and a runbook-skill upload example - opsbench should keep this as the canonical Cookbook reference its team-incident-response skill aligns to.
discovered.notes: Maintained by Anthropic; structure is exactly the subprocess-isolated MCP model opsbench already follows. Good for docs links.

discovered.name: alibabacloud/ack-mcp-server
discovered.fit_reason: Alibaba Cloud's container-service MCP server is a strong reference for how a cloud vendor wraps managed-K8s ops (similar surface to AKS) - opsbench team-platform-engineering can mirror its tool taxonomy when adding AKS/EKS/GKE parity to existing AKS-only recipes.
discovered.notes: Chinese-cloud focus limits direct use but the tool layout (cluster lifecycle, node-pool ops, audit logs) maps 1:1 to Azure AKS MCP surface opsbench targets.

discovered.name: WagnerAgent/awesome-mcp-servers-devops
discovered.fit_reason: Curated production-biased index of DevOps MCP servers covering source-control, IaC, K8s, CI/CD, cloud, observability, security - opsbench can mine it weekly to keep its recipe catalog discovering new tool surfaces without re-running broad searches.
discovered.notes: Pair with hammadhaqqani/awesome-devops-ai (459 tools, April 2026) for broader coverage.

discovered.name: stacklok/toolhive
discovered.fit_reason: Enterprise-grade platform for running and managing MCP servers as containers with policy enforcement - directly addresses opsbench's per-agent tool allowlist + Cedar gating needs by giving a managed runtime for the MCP servers it composes.
discovered.notes: Heavy infra but the policy/lifecycle model is the closest open-source analog to what opsbench is building; consider vendoring patterns even if not the full controller.

discovered.name: microsoft/mcp-gateway
discovered.fit_reason: Reverse-proxy and lifecycle manager for MCP servers in Kubernetes with session-aware routing - reference architecture for opsbench when it needs to multiplex many forensic MCP servers behind one allowlist-aware endpoint in AKS deployments.
discovered.notes: Microsoft-maintained, aligns with opsbench's AKS focus; could also be wrapped as the production deploy story.

discovered.name: Scoutflo/Scoutflo-SRE-Playbooks
discovered.fit_reason: Curated AWS+K8s SRE incident-response playbooks structured as step-by-step on-call troubleshooting guides - direct corpus opsbench team-incident-response can ingest as runbook skills (one playbook per skill markdown file).
discovered.notes: Plain-markdown content makes import trivial; verify each runbook before sealing as evidence-producing skill.

trends: The SRE-tooling AI ecosystem in 2026 has consolidated around MCP as the universal tool-surface protocol: every major incumbent (Robusta HolmesGPT, K8sGPT, Grafana, ArgoCD, PagerDuty, Rootly, Komodor, Headlamp) now ships a first-party MCP server, and CNCF has Sandbox-blessed both HolmesGPT and kagent as the reference agent runtimes. The frontier is moving from single-tool MCPs to multi-agent orchestration (Komodor Klaudia, Microsoft Azure SRE Agent GA April 2026, AWS DevOps Agent GA April 2026) with extensibility via MCP+OpenAPI, and to gateway/lifecycle layers (ToolHive, microsoft/mcp-gateway, kmcp) that manage fleets of MCP servers as Kubernetes-native resources. Read-only-by-default tool surfaces with explicit write-gate policies are emerging as the consensus security model.
gaps: There is no open-source agent toolkit that combines forensic-evidence sealing (SHA-256 chain-of-custody, immutable audit log), Cedar-policy gating, and per-agent MCP tool allowlists in one cohesive package - the major projects assume a trusted operator and ship neither evidence integrity nor policy-driven allowlists out of the box, which is precisely opsbench's unique wedge. There is also a gap in cross-MCP correlation: today's K8sGPT/HolmesGPT/Grafana/ArgoCD/PagerDuty MCPs are siloed, and no project provides a canonical "team-incident-response" recipe that composes them into a single forensic timeline with provenance tags. Finally, the platform-engineering side (catalog, scorecards, paved-road scaffolding) has no AI-agent layer beyond Backstage Q&A plugins, leaving room for an opsbench team-platform-engineering set of skills that wires Backstage/Cortex/Port into the same evidence-and-policy fabric.
